title: Nauclea pilulifera
taxonomy: Nauclea pilulifera belongs to the family Rubiaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. This plant is further classified as a member of the genus Nauclea, which includes several species of trees and shrubs native to tropical regions of Asia. The classification of Nauclea pilulifera is based on its morphology, anatomy, and molecular characteristics. The plant's classification is consistent with other members of the Rubiaceae family, which are known for their showy flowers and simple leaves. Nauclea pilulifera's classification is an important aspect of its identification and study in botany and ecology.
uses: Nauclea pilulifera is used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. It is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help in treating conditions such as fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The plant is also used to treat digestive problems, including diarrhea and dysentery, due to its astringent properties. In addition, Nauclea pilulifera is used in traditional medicine to treat wounds and skin conditions, including eczema and dermatitis. Its medicinal uses are largely based on its ability to promote healing and reduce inflammation.

constituents: Nauclea pilulifera contains a range of active constituents, including flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic compounds. The plant's alkaloid content is particularly notable, with compounds such as nauclea alkaloids and isoplatydesmine contributing to its therapeutic potential. Flavonoids present in Nauclea pilulifera include quercetin and kaempferol, which exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant's phenolic compound content includes caffeic acid and ferulic acid, among others. parts: Nauclea pilulifera, also known as the "African mahogany," has been traditionally used in various parts of the world for its medicinal properties. The bark of the plant is often utilized as a source of medicine, particularly for its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects. In traditional African medicine, the bark is used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. side_effects: Nauclea pilulifera has been reported to cause gastrointestinal disturbances, including nausea and diarrhea, due to its high tannin content. The tannins in this plant can also cause gastrointestinal irritation and may lead to stomach pain. In addition, excessive consumption of Nauclea pilulifera may result in kidney or liver problems due to the plant's potential toxicity. More research is needed to fully understand the side effects of Nauclea pilulifera, particularly its long-term effects on human health. intro: Nauclea pilulifera, also known as Quinine Tree or Quassia, is a plant species native to Southeast Asia and the Pacific Islands, belonging to the family Rubiaceae. The health benefits of Nauclea pilulifera include its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antimalarial properties, which have been used to treat various ailments such as fever, malaria, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of this plant include quassinoids, alkaloids, and flavonoids, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. In traditional medicine, Nauclea pilulifera is often prepared as a tea, extract, or capsule, and is also used as a raw material in the production of quassin, a compound used to treat malaria and other diseases.
